source-discipline.reason: All cited portal probes (NCC AU HTTP 200 42KB; openstd.samr.gov.cn returning 100KB; standards.govt.nz search 1MB no inline tokens; ANSI 403; ABNT 503; CrossRef GB-50763 returning IEEE ethernet) were performed live during this session, not pattern-matched from training. URLs and HTTP status codes recorded in §1.2-1.4 and §3.4 are observed, not invented.

best-practice-synthesis-routing.reason: This DR specifies verification methodology, not BPC synthesis content. No best_practice_synthesis written by Sonnet; the DR is methodology, which is Sonnet-eligible per rule #2's scope.

adversarial-research-protocol.reason: Rule #7 governs research-generating gap closures. This DR proposes a protocol; it does not close any gap row in the gaps table. The protocol itself does not assert empirical claims that would trigger rule #7's four-field requirement.

evidence-verification-gate-for-synthesis.reason: Rule #10 is the rule this DR operationalizes. §1.1 quantifies the rule #10 eligibility bottleneck; §3 specifies the new manual-verification track that writes to the rule #10 verification_status axis without modifying rule #10's predicate. The DR explicitly preserves rule #10 sub-rules 1/2/3 content-verification requirements (§5.5).

bias_direction: Strong self-authoring bias risk. This DR was authored in direct response to a sweep promise I made earlier in the same session that turned out to be infeasible. The bias direction is toward designing a protocol that makes the failed sweep retroactively feasible (i.e., toward optimism about manual-track yield, toward minimizing the SPA-coverage limitation, toward minimizing the single-reviewer error risk). I addressed this head-on in §5.1 but the surface area is large enough that an independent reviewer should specifically pressure-test §3.4's routing table and §3.2's UNVERIFIED-1 admission criterion.
independent_reviewer_counterclaim: An outside reviewer's strongest objection (acknowledged in §5.1): the worked example walks a single, unusually accommodating jurisdiction (AU, with a fully static government portal). It is not representative of the 55-row population, which is dominated by JP/CN/NL/NO/BR/SE — i.e., the harder SPA cases. The protocol's actual yield against this population is unknown until ≥3 jurisdictions are walked. A reviewer might further argue that admitting UNVERIFIED-1 as gate-passing for SPA-routed sources weakens rule #10's existence-verification semantics, since the SPA case verifies only that 'the body publishes something' rather than 'the specific cited document is fetchable.' The pilot gate in §6 step 3-4 is the partial mitigation; if the pilot fails, the protocol should be revoked rather than expanded.
